query: tipping of a tooth affects periodontal ligament

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The periodontal ligament fibers are torn when a tooth is avulsed, but the periodontal ligament cells remain viable on the root surface. Small and localized areas of cemental damage are usually seen due to the root scraping against the alveolar socket. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: For periodontal pocket reduction surgery, several local factors may reduce chances of regeneration, attachment gain, and pocket reduction, including root proximity, tipping or rotation of teeth, tooth mobility, thin mucosal tissue, the orientation of furcation entrances, enamel pearls, cervical enamel projections, denticles, ridges, root groves, and impacted teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The periodontal ligament (PDL) is the soft tissue connecting the cementum that covers the roots of the teeth to the surrounding alveolar bone. When an external impact occurs on a tooth, the periodontal fibers can tear, allowing partial or total displacement of the tooth from the socket. (see Image. Skin Laceration of Lower Lip and Chin After Trauma). The ensuing damage can lead to neuro-vascular disruption, as well as pulp necrosis. The maxillary central incisors are the most commonly affected teeth, followed by the maxillary lateral incisors. Often, more than one tooth is avulsed. Periodontal ligament fibers can desiccate rapidly in the open air. Even in a replanted tooth, the damaged periodontal ligament fibers can lead to bony resorption of the root. Root resorption will lead to crown fracture and loss of the tooth. [6] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Bone Defect Morphology
retrieved_docs.content: Horizontal bone defects refer to a generalized apical shift of the alveolar bone crest. When seen on x-rays, a distance greater than 1.9 mm from the cementoenamel junction (CEJ) indicates a high likelihood of bone and attachment loss. [10] Vertical defects typically appear as localized fading of the normally well-defined crestal bone, wedge-shaped areas of radiolucency adjacent to root surfaces, or multiple bone levels. Vertical defects are also known as intrabony defects. Tipping of teeth may cause wedge-shaped dips in the crestal bone as part of normal bone remodeling. But, unlike periodontal bone defects, the crestal bone will follow a line connecting the interproximal CEJ of adjacent teeth in such cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the physical characteristics of the root fragment to be removed, a surgeon may attempt all three of these principles - often simultaneously - until the root mobilizes. With time, the surgeon can predictably retrieve the root with forceps. A periotome is a handy instrument to use when desiring to preserve gingival and alveolar architecture. Manual and powered versions are available. The periotome uses a thin blade-like working end wedged within the periodontal ligament space, severing the ligamentous attachment encircling the root. The periotome avoids the need for excessive cortical bone removal. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Equipment -- Luxators
retrieved_docs.content: Luxators look similar to elevators; however, they are used with different aims. They are sharp-tipped instruments that should be used along the long axis of a tooth. These instruments aim to sever the periodontal ligament attachment to the tooth and to gradually widen the alveolar socket. [40] A luxator should be gradually advanced apically along a tooth, rotating side-to-side as it advances, allowing for as much apical mobility as possible. This gradual widening of the alveolar socket creates space for the application of instruments such as forceps, allowing for a more atraumatic extraction, which is beneficial for preserving bone for possible future implant placement. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Wedge Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Residual pocketing at a distal or mesial tooth surface that faces excessively thick gingival tissue Typically at the most distal tooth of the arch, adjacent to thick tissue of the maxillary tuberosity or the retromolar pad

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Periodontal ligament traction theory
retrieved_docs.content: According to this theory, the force that causes teeth to erupt is generated by the periodontal ligament-dental follicle complex, which exerts a pulling force through the traction power of fibroblasts when they contract. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Replantation
retrieved_docs.content: The tooth should be aligned anatomically and replanted, applying firm pressure to the socket. Most teeth can be successfully replanted if the extraoral dry time is less than 30 minutes; after this period, the survival probability of the tooth diminishes - the periodontal ligament cells are irreversibly damaged after 30 to 60 minutes.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Instruments -- Function -- Elevators
retrieved_docs.content: A variety of dental elevators exist to "lever" and mobilize teeth. They are used to apply forces to the tooth, severing the periodontal ligament and expanding the socket. Care is necessary not to apply so much force that damage is caused to surrounding structures, ultimately leading to iatrogenic injury, like alveolar fractures. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Knowledge of the basic structure of the teet is paramount to understand pathological processes and the logic behind current medical treatments. Below are some focal points of connection between histology and clinical significance. The periodontal ligament is the key part of malocclusion correction because it allows small progressive movements used by orthodontic techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Physiological changes can happen in teeth due to normal aging. However, changes can happen at different rates due to multiple factors like lifestyle, environment, and genetics that influence them. The changes in the appearance of teeth that occur over time are due to the wearing of the enamel, chipping, fracture lines, staining, exposure of dentin, and reduction in the size of the pulp chamber and root canals. These changes result in teeth that are generally darker in color. Also, periodontal support can modestly decrease with age, which results in gum recession on the buccal surface of teeth. With regular care, dentition should function effectively throughout a lifetime. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, sectioning the root may allow for segmental delivery. A hole can be drilled in the root to allow for a crane pick or similar instrument to lever the root coronally. [14] When used as a wedge between the root and supporting interradicular bone, the instrument’s tip is oriented parallel to the root, and a consistent application of apical pressure is transmitted. As the elevator continues apically, the periodontal ligament fibers begin to sever from the root, and the bony alveolus expands outward. A void forms between the walls of the socket and the root, allowing for a broader range of elevator tip motion and more alveolar expansion. Eventually, the force delivered will be greater than what the remaining periodontal ligament fibers can resist, allowing for the coronal displacement of the segment. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Instruments -- Function -- Periotomes and Luxators
retrieved_docs.content: These fine instruments are used to shear the periodontal ligament and act as a wedge to expand the socket. [6] [7] These are inserted between the tooth and socket and moved around the tooth to create space for the use of elevators and forceps while applying gentle pressure apically. [8] [9] A more atraumatic technique can be achieved by carefully widening a socket and making space for appropriate instruments, facilitating a good surface for implant placement through bone preservation. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Equipment -- Elevators
retrieved_docs.content: Elevators are instruments used partly to sever the periodontal ligament from the tooth to be extracted. They are also used to lift the tooth coronally out of the socket, sometimes resulting in the avulsion of the tooth or facilitating the application of forceps on the tooth. [38] They can also be used to complete sectioned roots and elevate roots out of the dental socket. Elevators should be applied perpendicular to the long axis of the tooth, with both parts of the elevator placed on hard surfaces. The inner aspect of the elevator tip should be placed on tooth tissue. The outer aspect of the elevator tip should be placed on the alveolar bone and not on the neighboring tooth. [39] This is because the elevation of the tooth relies on using the application point of the elevator as a fulcrum or a leverage point, which could inadvertently result in the movement of the neighboring tooth.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: As the invading oral microflora and the immune response clash, the periapical tissues surrounding the tooth's root, such as the periodontal ligament and alveolar bone, can be destroyed. This destruction causes periodontal lesions, which may vary in clinical manifestations. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injuries or internal injuries are associated with open wounds (جرح مفتوح), indicating that such traumatic forces often result in visible tissue disruption.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2430
retrieved_docs.content: Other miscellaneous procedures are classified as a subset of other nonoperative procedures, indicating that they fall under the broader category of nonoperative interventions.
